summary:
Lying down causes temporary pooling of blood in legs but compensatory mechanisms maintain stable venous return over time; thus, it does not lead to an instant significant rise in volume returning to heart compared to standing position.
The primary mechanism involves gravitational effects followed by physiological adjustments like vasoconstriction which help stabilize circulation upon reclining posture change from upright orientation without abrupt surge noted right away post-lie-down transition phase initiation. Summary:

Lying down leads to brief leg congestion before stabilization through adaptive responses such as increased vascular resistance, ensuring steady venous flow back to the heart instead of sudden increases immediately after sitting or standing positions are abandoned for recumbent ones. Key point here revolves around gradual adjustment processes countering potential drops in central venous pressure during decubitus positioning changes.
